vocabulary.notes: Kaixo is universal - used with friends, strangers, shopkeepers, anyone. More casual than egun on. Often followed by Zer moduz? (How are you?)
Agur sounds like the French bonjour but means goodbye. Do not confuse with the French greeting!
Bai is one of the first words visitors learn. Unlike Spanish si, it sounds like English bye!
Ez is also used as a prefix meaning not/un-: ezagutu=to know, ezezagun=unknown.

Eskerrik asko = many thanks. Esker = thanks + -rik (partitive suffix) + asko = much/many. The partitive -rik expresses an indefinite quantity of thanks. A beautiful construction: indefinitely many thanks.
Mesedez comes from Spanish por favor. Common in both formal requests and everyday politeness.

Barkatu = sorry or excuse me. Used both for apologies (Barkatu, oker nengoen = Sorry, I was wrong) and to get attention (Barkatu, non dago...? = Excuse me, where is...?). From Spanish perdonar via barka.

example.english: Excuse me where is the train?

vocabulary.notes: Zer moduz? = How are you? (literally how is the manner?). The standard everyday greeting after kaixo. Zer moduz zaude? is a slightly more emphatic form. Ondo = fine. Primeran = great. Txarto = badly.
Ondo = well or fine (adverb, not adjective). Ondo nago = I am well. Ondo egin = to do well. Ondo etorri = welcome (come well). Ongi = also means well (slightly more formal). Opposite: gaizki (badly).
Egun on literally means good day. Egun = day (same root as eguna). Used until around noon.

Gabon = good night (said when parting for the evening or bed). Also the Basque word for Christmas! Eguberri is another word for Christmas. Do not confuse with Gau on (good evening, used as a greeting when arriving somewhere).

Gau on = good evening (used as a greeting when arriving somewhere in the evening). Different from Gabon which is said when parting at night. Gau ona = good night (wishing someone a good night's sleep).
Ogia = bread, the staple of Basque meals. Ogia eta gazta = bread and cheese is a classic combination. Ogitegi = bakery (bread-place).
Ura is one of the shortest Basque words. Ur (water, no article) vs ura (the water).
Ardoa = wine, but the Basque Country is famous for Txakoli - a light sparkling white. Ardo beltza = red wine. Ardo zuria = white wine.
Sagardoa = cider (from sagar=apple + ardoa=wine). Basque cider houses (sagardotegi) are a winter tradition. Txotx! is the call to pour.

Arraina = fish. Central to Basque cuisine. Bakailaoa (salted cod), merluzea (hake) and besugoa (sea bream) are the most prized. The Basque fishing fleet was legendary - reaching Newfoundland centuries ago.
Jan = to eat, janari = food, jatekoa = something to eat. All from the same root jan.

Pintxoa = pintxo (plural: pintxoak). A small snack served on bread, skewered with a toothpick (pintxo). Similar to tapas but distinct - Basque pintxos are often elaborate works of art. Donostia is the pintxo capital.
Lunch is the main meal. Basque restaurants serve a menua (set lunch menu) - excellent value and quality.
Dinner in Basque culture is typically lighter than lunch. Afa comes from the verb afaldetu = to dine.

example.english: I will prepare dinner at home tonight.

vocabulary.notes: Tomatea in Basque cooking is everywhere - in pintxos, sauces, and the famous piperada (tomato and pepper stew). From Spanish tomate.
Oilaskoa = chicken. Oilasko errean = roast chicken. Oilasko saltsan = chicken in sauce. Oilasko salda = chicken broth. Very common on menus.

Gazta = cheese. Gatz = salt, so gazta literally means salted thing! Idiazabal is the famous smoked sheep's milk cheese from the Basque Country - PDO protected. Gazta freskoa = fresh cheese. Gaztagintza = cheesemaking.
Bat means one and also serves as the indefinite article. Etxe bat = a house. Bat, bi, hiru = one, two, three.
Bi also means both. Bietan = in both. Basque numbers are base-20: hogei=20, berrogei=40 (two twenties).
Hiru sounds like the English word ear. Hiru belarri = three ears - odd but memorable!
Lau sounds like the English word low. Four floors down = lau solairutan behera.
Bost is one of the most distinct Basque numbers - no relation to any other language.
Sei = six. Seina = six each. Seigarrena = sixth. Basque numbers: bat=1, bi=2, hiru=3, lau=4, bost=5, sei=6. Sei sounds like English "say" - helpful memory aid.
Zazpi - the double z is pronounced like English th (voiced). Practice: THATH-pee.
Zortzi contains the suffix -tzi which appears in 18 (hamazortzi) too. Pattern: learn it once.
Bederatzi is the longest single-digit number in Basque. Break it: beder + atzi.
Hamar = 10. Basque counts in 20s: hogei=20, hogeita hamar=30 (twenty and ten).
Hogei = 20. The base of the Basque vigesimal counting system. All round numbers are multiples of hogei: berrogei=40, hirurogei=60, laurogei=80. Hogeiren bat = about twenty. Hogei urte = twenty years.
Ehun = 100. Ehun eta bat = 101. Bi ehun = 200. Note: Basque uses base-20 so 400 is hogei ehun (twenty hundreds).
Mila = one thousand. Mila esker = a thousand thanks. Mila bider = a thousand times. Milioika = millions (of). Mila is also used as an intensifier: mila aldiz = countless times. Bi mila = 2000.
Ama is also used as a suffix: Euskal Ama = Mother Basque. Very commonly used in songs and poetry.
Aita is also used in religious contexts. Aita Gurea = Our Father (the Lords Prayer in Basque).

Anaia = brother (used by both men and women). Note: in some dialects women use neba for brother and anaia only for a female speaker's male sibling. Basque has distinct sibling terms by speaker gender.
Ahizpa = sister as used by a female speaker. A male speaker uses arreba for his sister. Basque uniquely distinguishes sibling terms by the gender of the speaker, not the sibling.

example.basque: Nire ahizpa eta anaia Donostian bizi dira.
example.english: My sister and brother live in Donostia.

vocabulary.notes: The -a at the end is the definite article. Seme = son (indefinite), semea = the son.
Alaba is also a province in the Basque Country (spelled Araba in Basque).
Amona combines ama (mother) + ona (good). Literally good mother - a lovely construction.
Aitona combines aita (father) + ona (good). Literally good father.

example.english: The grandfather is on the mountain.

vocabulary.notes: Familia comes from Latin/Spanish. The more traditional Basque word for family is etxea (house/home) - reflecting how family centred on the household.
Laguna = close friend or companion. Adiskidea is also used for friend. Lagunartea = circle of friends. Lagundu = to help or accompany (same root). Nire laguna = my friend.
Osaba = uncle. Izeba = aunt. These are the same word in many languages but Basque distinguishes them clearly.
Izeba = aunt (mothers or fathers sister). Do not confuse with osaba (uncle). Both are important in tight-knit Basque families.
All Basque colors end in -a (the definite article). Gorri = red, gorria = the red one.
Urdina is also used for green in some dialects. The sea (itsasoa) is described as urdina in poetry.
Berde comes from Latin viridis. Also used for traffic lights: berdea = go!
Zuri also means clean or pure. Ardi zuria = white sheep - a common Basque image.
Beltza is also a surname. The Basque flag (ikurrina) uses red, green and white - no black.
Hori also means that in Basque (hori da = that is it). Context makes the difference.
Same word as the fruit. Laranja comes from Arabic naranj via Spanish naranja.
Morea comes from Spanish morado. Also used for bruises - kolpe morea = a bruise.
Arrosa also means rose (the flower). A lovely double meaning - the color named after the flower. Arrosa bat = a rose.

example.english: I am wearing a pink shirt.

vocabulary.notes: Mendia is everywhere in Basque culture - the word Basque itself may derive from a root meaning mountains.
Basques have been master sailors for centuries. They fished cod in Newfoundland before Columbus arrived.
Ibaia is the river - critical in Basque geography. Bilbo (Bilbao) sits on the Ibaizabal river. Ibarra = valley (literally river-land).
The Basque Country (especially Bilbo (Bilbao)) is famous for rain. Euria egiten du = it is raining (literally rain does).

Eguzkia = the sun. Etymology debated - possibly from egun (day) + -zki, or possibly pre-Indo-European with no known root. The Basque sun deity was also called Eguzki - a female deity, unlike most solar gods.

example.basque: Euria gelditu da eta eguzkia ateratzen da.
example.english: The rain stopped and the sun is coming out.

vocabulary.notes: Elurra is important in Basque Country - the Pyrenees get heavy snow. Elurra egiten du = it is snowing.
Basoa means forest or wild. Basa-jaun (lord of the forest) is a famous Basque mythological figure - possibly the origin of the Basque surname Basajuan.
Haizea = the wind. Haize = wind (root). Haizearen = of the wind. The Basque Country coast is famous for strong Atlantic winds.
Lurra = the earth or ground. Lur = earth (root). Lurra jo = to hit the ground/fall. Lurra jan = to eat dirt (to fail badly).
Zuhaitza = tree. Zuhaitz = tree (root). The Gernikako Arbola (Tree of Gernika) is a sacred oak - symbol of Basque democracy.
Lorea = flower. Lore = flower (root). Lorategia = garden (flower-place). Loredun = flowery. Common in place names.
Buru also means mind or intelligence. Burutsua = clever (full of head). Buru onekoa = sensible.
Esku is the root. Eskua = the hand. Eskuak = hands. Eskubidea = right (literally hand-road)!
Oina = foot (specifically the foot, not the leg). Hanka = leg or foot more generally. Oinez = on foot/walking. Oinazea = pain/suffering. Oinetakoak = shoes (foot-things).
Begiak = eyes (plural). Begi = eye (root). Begiratu = to look/watch. Begi onez ikusi = to look favourably on. Begikoa = attractive.
Bihotz is used in many expressions. Bihotz oneko = kind-hearted. Bihotzez = heartfully/sincerely.
Ahoa = the mouth. Aho = mouth (root). Ahoa bete = a mouthful. Aho batez = unanimously (with one mouth). Ahoz = orally.
Sudurra = the nose. Sudur = nose (root). Sudur-zapi = handkerchief (nose-cloth). Sudurra sartu = to poke your nose in (to meddle).
Belarria = ear. Belarri = ear (root). Do not confuse with urtea (year) - similar sound! Belarria eman = to lend an ear.
Gaur is often the first time word learners pick up. Gaur goizean = this morning. Gaur arratsaldean = this afternoon.
Bihar also means need/necessity in some compound words. Biharko = for tomorrow.
Atzo is one of the more distinctive Basque words. Atzo arratsaldean = yesterday afternoon.
Orain often appears in political slogans. Orain Euskara! = Basque now! is a common language rights phrase.
Gero = later, then, or after. Gero eta gehiago = more and more. Eta gero? = And then? Gerora = for later. Lehenago = earlier. Geroago = later on. Uste gero = sooner or later.
Hilabetea = month. Hil = moon/death + bete = full. Literally a full moon cycle. Old Basque calendar was lunar.
Urtea = the year. Urte = year (root). Urteberri = New Year. Urtebete = one full year. Urtemuga = anniversary (year-limit).
Eguna = the day. Egun = day (root). Egunero = every day. Eguneroko = daily. Egunsentian = at dawn (day-birth).
Gaua = the night. Gau = night (root). Gauean = at night. Gaueko = of the night. Gauerdia = midnight (night-half).

Etxea = house or home. In Basque culture the etxe (family homestead) is central to identity - Basque surnames often derive from the family house name. Etxera = home (going home). Etxean = at home. Baserria = farmhouse.
Kalea = the street. Kale = street (root). Kalera = to the street. Kaletar = city-dweller. Bilbo (Bilbao)'s old town is full of narrow kaleak.
Hiria = city. Hiri = city (root). Hiritarra = city-dweller/urban. Hiriburua = capital city (city-head). Hiri handia = big city.
Trena = train. From Spanish tren. Euskotren is the Basque regional train network. Trena gelditzen da = the train stops.
Autobusa = bus. From Spanish autobús. Autobusera igo = to get on the bus. Pesa is the main Basque inter-city bus company.
Aireportua = airport. Aire = air + portu = port. Bilbo (Bilbao) airport is called Loiu. Donostia (San Sebastián) also has an airport.
Ostatua = hotel or inn. Ostat comes from old Basque meaning shelter. Also used for traditional rural guesthouses (casas rurales).
Jatetxea = restaurant. Jan = to eat + etxe = house. Eating house! Basque restaurants (jatetxeak) are world-renowned for their cuisine.
Poza = joy (noun). Pozik = happy (adjective). Poztu = to make happy. Zer poza! = What joy! One of the most positive Basque words.
Tristura is the noun form. Triste is the adjective (borrowed from Spanish/Latin). Tristura dut = I have sadness - note Basque uses have not am for emotions.
Beldurra dut = I am afraid (literally I have fear). Basque uses have (du) not be (naiz) for emotions. Beldurtu = to frighten.
Haserrea is the noun. Haserre is the adjective. Haserre nago = I am angry. Haserretu = to get angry. Very common emotional vocabulary.
Lana is one of the most common Basque words. Lan egin = to work. Langile = worker. Lantegia = factory/workplace.

example.basque: Lana gogorra da baina asebetetzea ematen du.
example.english: Work is hard but it is rewarding.

vocabulary.notes: Eskola comes from Greek via Latin schola. Ikastola is the Basque-language school - a key institution in language revival.
Liburua comes from Latin liber. Liburutegia = library (literally book-place). Books are central to Basque cultural identity.
Unibertsitatea = university. The University of the Basque Country (UPV/EHU) has over 45,000 students and teaches in both Basque and Spanish.
Euskara is the official name. The origin is mysterious - possibly from eus (hear/understand) + -kara (manner of).
Euskalduna defines identity through language, not blood. Anyone who speaks Basque is Euskaldun.

Euskal Herria = the Basque Country. Literally the Basque people's land (Euskal = Basque + Herria = people/land). Encompasses 7 territories across Spain and France. More than a geographic term - a statement of identity.
Ona = good. Hobea = better. Onena = best. Ondo = well (adverb). Ongi etorri = welcome (come well). Essential adjective.
Txarra = bad. Txarrena = worst. Txarto = badly (adverb). Do not confuse with gaizki (badly/wrongly). Txar = bad (root).
Handia is one of the most useful adjectives. Oso handia = very big. Handitu = to grow/enlarge. Handikeria = arrogance (being too big).
Txikia is the opposite of handia. Txikitu = to shrink/reduce. Txikitatik = from childhood (from being small). Very common word.
Zaharra = old (for things and people). Zahartze = aging. Do not confuse with antzinateko (ancient). Zahar = elder/old person (respected term).
Berria also means news! Berria is the name of the main Basque-language newspaper. Zer berri? = What is new? (What is the news?)
Ederra is the standard word for beautiful. Eder = beauty (root). Edertasun = beauty. Bai ederra! = How beautiful! Common exclamation.
Interesgarria = literally interest-having. The suffix -garri means causing/having. Interesting: interesa + garri. Harrigarri = amazing.
Pozik is the state of being happy. Poza = joy (noun). Poztu = to become happy. Oso pozik = very happy. Common everyday word.
Maite = love/beloved. Maite zaitut = I love you (the most important phrase!). Maitea = beloved/sweetheart. Maite izatea = to love someone.
Lasaia = calm. Lasaitu = to calm down. Lasai egon = stay calm. Lasaitasuna = calmness. Opposite of urduri (nervous).
Haserre = angry (adjective). Haserrea = anger (noun). Haserre nago = I am angry. Do not confuse with haserre (noun form).
Urduri = nervous/anxious. Urduritu = to become nervous. Urduria = nervousness. Very useful for expressing anxiety.
Harritu = to surprise or to be surprised (verb). Harrituta nago = I am surprised (literally I am astonished). Harrigarria = amazing/astonishing. Harri = stone - as if turned to stone with surprise.

Ilusioa = excited anticipation or enthusiasm (from Spanish ilusión). FALSE FRIEND: does not mean illusion in English. Ilusioa daukat = I am excited/I am looking forward to it. Very common in everyday speech.
Lotsa = shame or embarrassment. Lotsatu = to be embarrassed. Lotsagarria = shameful. Lotsagabea = shameless (without shame).
Zer berri? = What is new? Berri = news/new. The same word means both. Berria = the news/something new.
Mila esker = a thousand thanks. Mila = thousand. Much more emphatic than eskerrik asko. Used for genuine gratitude.
Saltsa = sauce. Basque sauces are legendary: salsa verde (green sauce with parsley), pil-pil (emulsified cod sauce), bizkaina. From Spanish salsa.
Txakolina = txakoli wine. A light, slightly sparkling, very dry white wine made from unripe grapes. Poured from height to aerate it. Produced in Gipuzkoa, Bizkaia and Araba. Perfect with pintxos.
Bakailaoa = salted cod, the cornerstone of Basque cuisine. Pil-pil sauce, al pil-pil, bakailao a la vizcaina - all famous dishes. Euskara Batua spelling is bakailaoa, from Spanish bakailao.
Goxoa = delicious or sweet. The x sounds like sh (go-SHO-ah). Goxoki = candy/sweet. Goxotasuna = sweetness. Oso goxoa! = So delicious! Used constantly in Basque food culture.
Musika = music. Musikaria = musician. Musikal = musical. Basque traditional music uses the txalaparta (wooden percussion) and trikitixa (accordion).
Dantza = dance. Dantzaria = dancer. Aurresku is the most famous traditional Basque dance - performed at festivals and weddings.

Festa = festival or party. Every Basque town has its annual festak. The most famous is San Fermin in Iruña (Iruña (Pamplona)) with the running of the bulls. Aste Nagusia in Bilbo (Bilbao) is another huge celebration.

example.basque: Urtero festa handia egiten dugu herrian.
example.english: Every year we hold a big festival in town.

vocabulary.notes: Pilota = pelota or jai alai. One of the fastest ball sports in the world. Pilotaria = player. Frontoia = court. Played with bare hand, bat or basket.

Improvised sung verse in strict metre and rhyme. Bertsolaritza is the art of improvising these verses - competitions (txapelketak) fill sports arenas. A living oral tradition unique to Basque culture.

Sukaldaritza = cuisine or the art of cooking. Sukaldaria = chef. Sukaldea = kitchen. Basque cuisine is world-renowned with more Michelin stars per capita than anywhere on earth. Arzak, Berasategui, and Mugaritz lead globally.

The Basque beret - a symbol of cultural identity worn by men and women. Txapelketa = championship (literally beret-contest - the winner receives a txapela). Comes in many colours though black is traditional.
Errialdea = territory or region. Euskal Herria has 7 historical territories (herrialeak) - 4 in Spain, 3 in France.
Bidea = path, way, road, or method/means. Bide eman = to allow/give way. Bidegabea = unjust (without a way). Autobidea = motorway. Burubidea = idea/concept (head-way).
Mapa = map. From Spanish mapa. Euskal Herriko mapa = map of the Basque Country. Mapagilea = cartographer.
Geltokia = station. Gelditu = to stop - so geltokia is literally the stopping place. Autobus geltokia = bus station.
Kotxea comes from Spanish coche. Kotxea aparkatu = to park the car. Garaje = garage. Auto is also used in informal speech.
Itsasontzia = ship (itsaso=sea + ontzia=vessel). Basque sailors were among the first to fish Newfoundland cod before Columbus reached America.
Bidaia = journey or trip. Bidaiari = traveller. Bidaiatu = to travel. Bide = road/way - so bidaia is literally a going on the road.

example.basque: Bidaia luzea da baina merezi du.
example.english: The journey is long but it is worth it.

vocabulary.notes: Osasuna = health. Also the name of the Iruña (Pamplona) football club - a healthy team! Osasun ona = good health. Osasuntsu = healthy.

example.english: You need to take care of your health.

vocabulary.notes: Sendagilea = doctor. Sendatu = to heal. Sendabidea = remedy/cure (healing-path). Sendagile is a beautiful word: the one who heals.
Ospitalea = hospital. From Latin hospitale. Basque public healthcare (Osakidetza) is among the best in Europe.
Arnasa = breath. Arnasa hartu = to take a breath/breathe in. Arnasgune = breathing space. Arnastu = to breathe. Used in meditation.
Irakaslea = teacher. Irakatsi = to teach. Irakaskuntza = education/teaching. Ikastola teachers helped revive Basque in the 20th century.
Ikaslea = student. Ikasi = to learn. Ikasgela = classroom (learning-room). Ikastola = Basque-language school. All from root ika- (to learn).
Bulegoa = office. From Spanish bufete/despacho via French bureau. Bulegokidea = office colleague. Bulegolana = office work.
Bilera = meeting. Bileratu = to meet/gather. Bilera batzordea = committee meeting. Bilerara joan = to go to a meeting.
Langilea = worker. Lan = work + -gile = maker/doer. Langilegoa = the working class. Langile mugimendu = labour movement. Central to Basque industrial history.
Astelehenea = Monday. Aste = week + lehena = first. Literally the first of the week. Work starts here for most Basques.
Asteartea = Tuesday. Aste = week + artea = middle/between. Literally between-the-week. The second day.

Asteazkena = Wednesday. Aste = week. The second element is debated - possibly from Latin origins or aste erdi (mid-week) rather than azkena (last). Asteazkenean = on Wednesday. The hardest day name to remember.
Osteguna - the days of the week are among the hardest Basque words to remember. Try: Aste (week) + lehena=Monday, artea=Tuesday, azkena=Wednesday...
Ostirala = Friday. Origin uncertain - possibly from Latin. TGIF in Basque: Ostirala da! Ostiraleko = of Friday.
Larunbata = Saturday. Etymology uncertain - possibly from Latin Saturni dies (day of Saturn) like English Saturday. Larunbatean = on Saturday. Larunbatero = every Saturday.
Igandea = Sunday. Igan = to ascend/rise. The day of rising - reflecting its religious significance. Igandero = every Sunday.
Goizean = in the morning. Goiz = morning (root). Goizeko = of the morning. Goizero = every morning. Goiz jaiki = to get up early.

example.english: I woke up in the morning.

vocabulary.notes: Arratsaldean = in the afternoon. Arratsalde = afternoon/evening. Arratsaldeko = of the afternoon. Arratsaldero = every afternoon.
Senarra = husband. Senar = husband (root). Senarreba = in-laws. Bizilagun eta senarra = neighbor and husband (sometimes the same).
Emaztea = wife. Emazte = wife (root). Emaztearen = wife's. An old word - modern Basque also uses bikotekidea (partner) more neutrally.
Bikotea = couple or partner. Bikote = pair (root). Bikotekidea = partner/member of a couple. Bikotean = as a couple.
Bizilaguna = neighbor. Bizi = to live + laguna = friend. Literally living-friend - what a lovely way to describe a neighbor!

Herria = town, people, or nation. Herri = people/nation (root). Euskal Herria = the Basque homeland. Herritar = citizen. Herrialde = country/territory. One of the most powerful words in Basque political discourse.
Hizkuntza = language. Hizkuntzalari = linguist. Hizkuntza politika = language policy. Euskara is the hizkuntza of the Basques.
Kultura = culture. Kulturala = cultural. Kultura etxea = cultural centre. Basque culture (euskal kultura) is distinct and ancient.
Historia = history. Historikoa = historic. Historiagilea = historian. Basque history stretches back thousands of years.

Askatasuna = freedom or liberty. Aske = free (root). Askatu = to free/liberate. Askatasunez = freely. Askatasunaren aldeko = in favour of freedom. A central concept in Basque political and cultural life.
Elkartasuna = solidarity. Elkartu = to unite/join together. Elkar = each other. Elkartasunez = with solidarity. Key Basque labor movement word.
Argia = light or bright. Also means clever/smart! Oso argia = very clever. Argi = light (root). Argitu = to enlighten. Argipen = explanation.
Iluna = dark. Ilundu = to get dark. Ilunabar = dusk/twilight (dark-edge). Iluntasun = darkness. Ilunpean = in the dark/in secret.
Azkarra = fast or sharp. Azkar ibili = to move fast. Oso azkarra = very sharp/clever. Also means quick-witted. A compliment in Basque culture.
Motela = slow. Motel = slowly (adverb). Moteldu = to slow down. Also used for dull/boring people: oso motela = very boring.
Garestia = expensive. Garesti = expensively. Do not confuse with aberatsa (rich/wealthy). Garestitu = to become expensive.
Merkea = cheap or affordable. Merke = cheaply. Merkatua = market (from Latin mercatus, same root as English merchant). Merketu = to become cheaper. Opposite: garestia (expensive).
Erraza = easy. Erraz = easily (adverb). Erraztasuna = ease/facility. Erraztu = to make easier. Opposite of zaila (difficult).
Zaila = difficult. Zaildu = to become harder. Zailtasuna = difficulty. Zailtasunak gainditu = to overcome difficulties.
Ozena = loud. Ozen = loudly. Ozendu = to get louder. Ozentasuna = loudness. Do not confuse with garrantzitsua (important).
Isila = quiet or silent. Isilik = quietly. Isildu = to go silent. Isilpean = in secret (under silence). Isiltasuna = silence.
Marroina = brown. From Spanish marrón. The traditional Basque word is kastainu (chestnut-colored). Used for hair, earth, and wood.
Grisa = grey. From French gris. Basque Country skies are often grisa - the region averages 150+ rainy days per year!
Zakurra = dog. Zakur = dog (root). Zakurtegi = kennel. The Basque Country has its own dog breeds including the Basque Shepherd (Euskal Artzain Txakurra).
Katua = cat. Katu = cat (root). From Latin cattus. Katuak bederatzi bizia ditu = cats have nine lives (same saying in Basque!).
Iragana = the past. Iraganeko = of the past. Iragana ezin da aldatu = the past cannot be changed. Key word in Basque history discussions.

The future. Etorkizuneko = future (adjective). Etorkizunean = in the future. Key word in Basque language planning - etorkizuna and the survival of Euskara is a constant political discussion. From etorri (to come) + -kizun (that which will).
Aldia = a period or turn. Nire aldia da = it is my turn. Aldi berean = at the same time. Noizean behin = from time to time (literally at some period).
Ezkontza = wedding. Ezkondu = to marry. Ezkontide = spouse. Ezkongai = engaged person. Basque weddings are famous multi-day celebrations.

Border area or frontier zone. Muga = border + aldea = side/area. The French-Spanish border runs through the Basque Country - the Basque people straddle it. Mugakide = border neighbour. A politically sensitive concept.

Route or itinerary. Ibil = walk/travel + bidea = path. Literally the travelling-path. The Camino de Santiago (Santiago Bidea) passes through the Basque Country via Donostia. GR11 and GR10 are famous Basque hiking routes.
Natura = nature. Natural = natural. Naturatik = from nature. Basques have a deep connection to natura - mountains, sea, and forests are central to identity.
Ingurumena = environment. Inguru = surroundings (root). Ingurumen politika = environmental policy. Ingurugiroa = natural environment (more specific).
Lanbidea = profession. Lan = work + bide = path. Literally the work-path. What is your profession? = Zein da zure lanbidea?
Soldata = salary or wage. From Spanish sueldo. Soldata igokera = pay rise. Gutxieneko soldata = minimum wage.
Enpresa = company. Enpresaria = entrepreneur. Enpresa txikia = small business. Basque Country has a strong tradition of cooperatives (Mondragon).
Erakundea = organization. Eratu = to form/organize (root). Erakunde publikoa = public institution. Erakunde soziala = social organization.
Industria = industry. Industriala = industrial. Bilbo (Bilbao) was once a major steel and shipbuilding centre - industry shaped modern Basque identity.

Championship. Txapela = beret + -keta = contest. The Bertsolari Championship (Bertsolari Txapelketa) held every 4 years fills the BEC arena in Bilbo (Bilbao) with 15,000 fans watching improvised verse. A unique Basque institution.

Bertsolaria = verse singer (bertsolaritza practitioner). Improvises in strict metre and rhyme on given themes. National championships fill the BEC arena in Bilbo (Bilbao) with 15,000 spectators. A uniquely Basque art form.
Ohitura = custom or habit. Ohitu = to get used to. Ohitura onez = by good custom. Ohiturak eta balioak = customs and values.
Erritu = ritual. Errituzko = ritual/ceremonial. Basque culture has many ancient rituals tied to seasons, agriculture and community life.
Kezkatu = worried. Kezka = worry/concern (root). Kezkatzea = worrying. Kezkagarria = worrying/concerning. Kezkatu naiz = I am worried.
Harro = proud. Harrotasun = pride. Harrotu = to become proud. Harro egon = to be proud of. Oso harro nago = I am very proud.
Esperantza = hope. From Spanish esperanza. The Basque word itxaropena is also used (from itxaron = to wait). Esperantza dugu = we have hope.
Adorea = courage. Adoretsu = courageous. Adoretu = to encourage. Adorea hartu = take courage. From Spanish adorar via ador = honor.
Larrituta = stressed or upset. Larritu = to become worried/stressed. Larritasuna = anxiety/distress. Larria = serious/grave (root).
Beroa = hot or warm. Bero = heat (root). Berotasuna = warmth. Berokia = coat/jacket (literally the warming thing). Berogailua = heater.
Hotza = cold. Hotz = cold (root). Hotzikara = shiver (cold-shake). Hotzikaraz = with shivers. Hotz egin = to feel cold. Opposite: beroa.
Garbia = clean or pure. Garbitu = to clean. Garbitasuna = cleanliness/purity. Garbiketa = cleaning. Garbi hitz egin = to speak clearly.
Gozoa = sweet or delicious. Gozoki = candy/sweet treat. Gozotasuna = sweetness. Gozatu = to enjoy. Gozamenak = pleasures.
Gazia = salty. Gatz = salt (root). Gatzontzi = salt cellar. Gazta = cheese (literally salted thing!). Itsasoa gazia da = the sea is salty.
Osoa = whole or complete. Osotasuna = wholeness/completeness. Osotu = to complete. Oro = all + -soa. Oso = very (adverb from same root).
Normala = normal. Normalean = normally/usually. Normalizazioa = normalization (of Basque language use). Key political term.
Indartsu = strong. Indar = strength/force (root). Indarra = the force. Indartu = to strengthen. Indar handia = great strength.
Hizkuntza politika = language policy - central to Basque politics. Official language status, education medium, and public signage are all debated.
Normalizazioa = normalization - specifically the process of making Basque normal in daily life. A key political and cultural concept.
Gizarte mugimendua = social movement. Gizarte = society + mugimendua = movement. Basque Country has a rich history of social movements.
Gizartea = society. Gizon = man/human + -arte = between. Literally between-humans. Gizarteratu = to socialize. Gizarte zerbitzuak = social services.
Ekonomia = economy. Ekonomikoa = economic. The Basque Country has one of the highest GDPs per capita in Spain - a strong industrial economy.
Boterea = power or authority. Boteretsu = powerful. Boteredun = powerful person. Botere = power (root). Central to Basque political vocabulary.
Justizia = justice. Justiziazkoa = just/fair. Injustizia = injustice. From Latin justitia. Basque legal system (foral law) has ancient roots.
Abertzalea = patriot (from aberri=homeland + -zalea=lover of). Aberri Eguna = Basque Homeland Day. Can be political - use contextually.
Herritartasuna = citizenship. Herritar = citizen/townsperson. Herri = town/people. Herritartasun agiria = citizenship document.

Nazionalismoa = nationalism. A central concept in Basque politics. Abertzaletasuna is the specifically Basque nationalist term. Nazionalismo euskalduna = Basque nationalism. Ranges from cultural to independence-seeking.
Autodeterminazioa = self-determination. A core concept in Basque politics. The right of Basques to decide their own political future.
Giza eskubideak = human rights. Giza = human + eskubideak = rights (literally hand-paths). Giza eskubideen adierazpena = Declaration of Human Rights.
Ondarea = heritage or legacy. Ondare = inheritance (root). Kultura ondarea = cultural heritage. Basque heritage includes language, sport, music and food.

Identity - central to Basque cultural and political life. Euskal identitatea = Basque identity. The question of who is Basque (language-based vs blood-based) is actively debated. Basque identity is largely defined through language use.
Tradizioa = tradition. Tradizional = traditional. Tradizioak gorde = to preserve traditions. Basque traditions are unusually well-preserved.

example.basque: Gure tradizioak bizirik daude belaunaldiz belaunaldi.
example.english: Our traditions are alive from generation to generation.

vocabulary.notes: Modernitatea = modernity. Moderno = modern. The tension between modernitatea and tradizioa is a constant theme in Basque cultural debate.

Euskara Batua = Unified Basque. Standardised form created by the Academy (Euskaltzaindia) in 1968 at Arantzazu. Before this, Basque had no standard written form. Batua = unified (from batu = to unite). Now taught in all schools.
Euskalkia = dialect. Basque has 5-7 main dialects: Bizkaiera, Gipuzkera, Lapurtera, Zuberera, Nafarrera. Unified Basque (Euskara Batua) was standardised in 1968.
Klima aldaketa = climate change. Klima = climate + aldaketa = change. Aldatu = to change. Urgent issue given Basque Country's coastal geography.

example.english: Climate change is a serious problem.

vocabulary.notes: Enpresaria = entrepreneur. Basque Country has the Mondragon Cooperative Corporation - one of the world's largest worker-owned cooperatives. Entrepreneurship is valued.

Greba = labour strike. Grebara joan = to go on strike. Greba orokorra = general strike. The Basque Country has a strong union tradition - the metalworkers of Bilbo (Bilbao) were historically militant.
Produktibitatea = productivity. Produktibo = productive. The Basque economy is known for high productivity, especially in manufacturing and industry.
Epea = deadline or period. Epe = period (root). Epemuga = deadline (period-limit). Epe laburrean = in the short term. Very common in work contexts.

Iraungitzea = expiry or expiration. Iraungitu = to expire. Iraungitze data = expiry date. Used for passports, contracts, food, medicine. Iraungi da = it has expired. Iraungitu gabe = not yet expired. From iraun (to last) + -gitu.
Estatistika = statistics. Estatistikoa = statistical. Eustat is the official Basque statistics agency. Data-driven governance is common in the region.
Mintzatu and hitz egin both mean to speak, but mintzatu is more formal/literary. Hitz egin is everyday speech.
Ulertu = to understand. Ulertzen dut = I understand. Ulertu al duzu? = Did you understand? Ulertzeko erraza = easy to understand. Ulerterraz = comprehensible. Ulertezina = incomprehensible.

Ikasi = to learn or study. Ikasten ari naiz = I am studying. Ikasle = student (one who learns). Ikasketa = study/studies. Ikasgela = classroom (study room). Ikastola = Basque-medium school. This app is named Ikasi & Go!
Lagundu = to help or accompany. Laguna = friend (root). So to befriend someone is to help them. Lagunduko dizut = I will help you.
Aukeratu = to choose. Aukera = option/opportunity (root). Aukeratze = choosing. Zein aukeratzen duzu? = Which do you choose?
Aldatu = to change. Aldaketa = change (noun). Aldakorra = changeable. Dena aldatu da = everything has changed. Very common verb.

example.basque: Hiriak asko aldatu da azken urteetan.
example.english: The city has changed a lot in recent years.

vocabulary.notes: Jarraitu = to continue or follow. Jarraipen = continuation. Jarraikia = follower. Jarraitu ezazu = please continue. Also used for following someone.
Saiatu = to try. Saiakera = attempt/essay. Saialdia = try/attempt. Saiatu naiz = I tried. Very encouraging word - keep trying!
Pentsatu = to think. Pentsamendu = thought/idea. Pentsalari = thinker/philosopher. Zer pentsatzen duzu? = What do you think?
Sentitu = to feel. Sentimendu = feeling/emotion. Sentikorra = sensitive. Sentitzen dut = I feel / I am sorry. Dual meaning!
Lortu = to achieve or get. Lortu dut! = I did it! / I got it! Lortu ezin = cannot be achieved. Lorpen = achievement. Very satisfying word to use.

example.english: It was hard but you achieved it!

vocabulary.notes: Eman = to give. Eman iezadazu = give it to me. Esku eman = to shake hands (give hand). Adore eman = to encourage (give courage). Beldurra eman = to frighten (give fear). One of the most idiomatic Basque verbs.
Hartu = to take or receive. Hartzaile = receiver. Harrera = reception/welcome. Hartu-eman = give and take. One of the most common Basque verbs.
Etorri = to come. Etorri naiz = I have come. Etorriko naiz = I will come. Etorri zara? = Have you come? / Did you come? Ongi etorri = welcome!
Joan = to go. Nora joaten zara? = Where are you going? Joango naiz = I will go. Joan-etorri = return trip (go-come). Essential verb.
Ikusi = to see. Ikusle = spectator/viewer. Ikusgarria = spectacular (worth seeing). Ikusiko dugu = we will see. Ikusi arte = see you later!
Esan vs mintzatu: esan = to say something specific (esan dit = he told me), mintzatu = to speak in general.

example.english: What do you want to say?

vocabulary.notes: Jakin = to know. Jakintsu = wise/knowledgeable. Jakinduria = wisdom/knowledge. Ez dakit = I do not know. Jakinarazi = to inform/notify.
Nahi = to want/will. Nahi dut = I want. Nahi gabe = unwillingly. Nahikoa = enough (as much as wanted). Nahi eta nahi ez = whether you want or not.
Behar = need/must. Behar dut = I need/I must. Zer behar duzu? = What do you need? Beharrezkoa = necessary. One of the most used words in Basque.

example.english: It is late, I must go.

vocabulary.notes: Aberatsa = rich or wealthy. Aberastasuna = wealth. Aberastu = to become rich. Aberats = wealthy (root). Do not confuse with nagusia (boss/employer).
Pobrea = poor. From Spanish pobre/Latin pauper. The Basque word txiroa is also used. Pobrezia = poverty. Pobretu = to become poor.
Zuzena = correct, just, or straight. Zuzendu = to correct/direct. Zuzentasuna = justice/correctness. Zuzenean = directly/live (as in live broadcast).
Okerra = wrong or crooked. Oker = wrong/crooked (root). Okertzen = going wrong. Do not confuse with gaizki (badly). Okerrena = the worst.
Garrantzia = importance. Garrantzitsua = important. Garrantzitsuena = the most important. Very commonly heard in news and formal speech.
Eragina = influence or effect. Eragin = to cause/influence (root). Eraginkorra = effective. Eragingarria = influential. Zer eragin du? = What effect did it have?
Helburua = goal or objective. Heldu = to arrive/reach + buru = head. Literally the head you are heading to. Helburua lortu = to achieve the goal.
Aukera = opportunity or option. Aukeratu = to choose (verb from this root). Aukera ona = good opportunity. Aukerak eta baliabideak = opportunities and resources.
Arazoa = problem. Arazo = problem (root). Arazotsua = problematic. Arazoak konpondu = to solve problems. Arazorik ez = no problem!
Konponbidea = solution. Konpondu = to fix/solve (root) + bide = path. Literally the fixing-path. Konponbide egokia = appropriate solution.
Baldintza = condition or requirement. Baldintzatu = to condition/restrict. Baldintzarik gabe = unconditionally. Lan baldintzak = working conditions.
Ondorioa = consequence or conclusion. Ondorio = consequence (root). Ondoriozta = to conclude. Hortaz ondoriozta dezakegu = from this we can conclude.
Nola? = How? One of the most essential question words. Nola duzu izena? = What is your name? (literally how do you have your name?) Nola joan? = How to go? Nola esaten da? = How do you say it?

Non? = Where? Essential for navigation. Non dago? = Where is it? Non bizi zara? = Where do you live? Non jaio zinen? = Where were you born? Nondik = from where? Nora = to where? Basque distinguishes location (non) from direction (nora).
Noiz? = When? Noiz etorriko zara? = When will you come? Noiz arte? = Until when? Noizbehinka = occasionally (sometimes-when). Noizean behin = from time to time. Noiznahi = whenever.
Zer? = What? The most common question word in Basque. Zer da hori? = What is that? Zer nahi duzu? = What do you want? Zer moduz? = How are you? (what is the manner?). Zer berri? = What's new?
Nor? = Who? Nor zara zu? = Who are you? Nor da? = Who is it? Nork = by whom (ergative case). Nori = to whom. Basque has 4 cases for who!
Zergatik = why. Zergati = reason (root). Zergatik ez? = Why not? One of the most useful question words for understanding explanations.
Zenbat? = How many? / How much? Zenbat da? = How much does it cost? Zenbat urte dituzu? = How old are you? Zenbat denbora? = How long? Zenbat eta gehiago... = The more...
To be (location/state) or to stay. Dago = he/she is. Nago = I am.

Ibili = to walk or go around. Ibiltzen naiz = I am walking. Nora zoaz ibiltzen? = Where are you going for a walk? Ibilaldi = walk/outing. Ibilgailua = vehicle (a walking thing). Ibili is also in this app's name: Ikasi (learn) and go (ibili)!

Egin = to do or make. One of the most versatile Basque verbs - used in hundreds of compounds. Lan egin = to work. Hitz egin = to speak. Barre egin = to laugh. Negar egin = to cry. Egin dezagun = let's do it!
Eraman = to carry or take somewhere. Eramango dut = I will take it. Eramaile = carrier/bearer. Do not confuse with hartu (to take/receive).

vocabulary.english: To talk / To have a conversation
vocabulary.notes: Hitz egin (to speak) vs mintzatu (to speak formally). Hitz literally means word - so hitz egin = to do words!
Bizi = to live. Bizi naiz = I am alive / I live. Non bizi zara? = Where do you live? Bizirik = alive. Bizitza = life/livelihood.
Pozgarria = joyful or pleasing. Poz = joy + -garri (causing). Literally joy-causing. Similar formation: harrigarria (astonishing), nekagarria (tiring).
Harrigarria = amazing or astonishing. Harri = stone + -garri. Turned to stone with amazement. Zer harrigarria! = How amazing!

Gogorra = hard or tough, physically or emotionally. Lan gogorra = hard work. Bizia gogorra da = life is tough. Do not confuse with zaila (difficult/hard in terms of complexity). Gogortasuna = hardness/toughness.
Alaia = cheerful or joyful. Alaitasuna = cheerfulness/joy. Alaigarri = cheering/uplifting. Alai ibili = to go about cheerfully. A lovely warm word.
Hemen = near speaker. Hor = near listener. Han = away from both. This 3-way distinction is unique among European languages.
Han is used for things far from both speakers. Hango = from there. Haraino = up to there. The root appears in many place names.
Basque has 3 heres/theres based on distance: hemen (near me), hor (near you), han (away from both). Like Japanese ko/so/a!
Beti = always. Betiere = always/forever. Betiko = eternal/permanent. Beti bezala = as always. Beti maite = always beloved.

Inoiz = ever (questions/conditionals) or never (with ez). Inoiz joan zara? = Have you ever gone? Inoiz ez = never. Also used before superlatives: inoiz egin den film onena = the best film ever made. Do not confuse with sekula (never, emphatic).

example.english: Have you ever been to Bilbo?

vocabulary.notes: Sekula = never (emphatic). Sekula ez dut ikusi = I have never ever seen it. More emphatic than inoiz ez. From Latin saecula (ages/forever).
Essential word for any traveller. Komunak non daude? = Where are the toilets?
Taxi. In Basque x = sh sound, so taxia sounds like TAH-shee-ah not "taxi". Taxi bat nahi dut = I want a taxi. Taxis are widely available in Basque cities.
Beer. Garagar (barley) + ardoa (wine) = literally barley-wine. The Basque brewing tradition is ancient. Garagardo bat mesedez = one beer please. Very popular in pintxo bars alongside txakoli.
The menu, but also refers to the set lunch menu (menua) which is excellent value in Basque restaurants.
The bill. Faktura mesedez = The bill please. Also used for invoices in business.
Left direction. Ezkerrera = to the left. Ezkerra also means left-wing in politics.
Right direction. Eskuinera = to the right. Eskuina also means right-wing in politics.
Straight ahead or correct. Zuzen-zuzen = perfectly straight. Also means just/fair.
Open. Irekita dago = it is open. Do not confuse with itxita (closed).
Closed. Itxita dago = it is closed. Do not confuse with irekia (open).
Help or assistance. Laguntza! = Help! Laguntza behar dut = I need help.
Police. In the Basque Country the local police are called Ertzaintza.

example.english: I need to call the police.

vocabulary.notes: Legs (plural). Singular: hanka. Hanka min dut = my leg hurts.
The back. Bizkar min dut = I have back pain - a very common phrase.
Sabela = stomach or belly. Sabel min dut = I have a stomach ache. Sabelalde = abdominal area. Sabelaldi = stomachful. Do not confuse with maskuria (bladder). Sabela bete = full stomach.
Teeth (plural). Singular: hortza. Hortz min dut = I have toothache.
Silver color. Zilar also means silver the metal. Zilarrezko = made of silver.
Hamar (10) + bi (2) = 12. Basque builds teen numbers as ten+number. Hamahiru=13, hamalau=14, hamabost=15.
Hamar (10) + bost (5) = 15. A key number - used in telling time (quarter past/to).
Hogei (20) + ta (and) + hamar (10) = 30. Basque is vigesimal - all numbers built from 20s.
Lehena = first. Bigarrena = second. Hirugarrena = third. Ordinals use -garren suffix after 1st.

One o'clock. Ordu bietan = at two o'clock. Zer ordu da? = What time is it? Ordu also means appointment: ordu bat hartu = to make an appointment. Ordu erdia = half past. Ordutegia = timetable/schedule.

example.english: We will meet at one o'clock.

vocabulary.notes: Erdia = half. Ordu bata eta erdia = half past one (literally one and a half). Ordu biak eta erdia = half past two. Eta = and links the hour and erdia (half).

A full phrase: Pozten nau zu ezagutzeak = Knowing you pleases me = Nice to meet you. Poztu = to please. Nau = me (it pleases me). Zu = you. Ezagutzeak = knowing (verbal noun). This grammatically complex phrase is worth memorising whole as an essential social expression.
Nongo = of where + -a (article) = where-from-person. Nongoa naiz = I am from...
Literally how many years do you have? Basque uses have not be for age - Hogei urte ditut = I am twenty.
Business or a deal. Negozio ona = good business. Negozioak egin = to do business. From Spanish negocio.
Competition (between businesses) or competence (skill). Context determines meaning.
Budget or budget allocation. Aurrekontu mugatua = limited budget. Key in business and government.
Investment. Inbertitu = to invest. Inbertitzaile = investor. The Basque Country attracts significant foreign investment.
Egg. Arrautzak = eggs (plural). Arrautza frijitua = fried egg. Arrautza opila = omelette. Essential in Basque cooking.
Milk. Esne = milk (root). Esne-beltza = black coffee with a drop of milk. Esne-gaina = cream. Common in traditional Basque breakfasts.
Coffee. The Basque word for a coffee with milk is 'esne-kafea' or 'cortado'. Kafea hartu = to have a coffee.

example.english: I want to have a coffee.

vocabulary.notes: Tea. Also written tee. Less common than coffee in the Basque Country but widely available.
Opila is a traditional Basque sweet bread eaten at Easter. Tarta = cake in general usage.
Fruit. Frutak = fruits (plural). The Basque Country is known for its apples (sagarrak) used to make sagardoa (cider).
Arm. Beso = arm (root). Besarkada = hug (arm-full). Besoa luzatu = to extend the arm.
Leg or foot. Hanka = leg/foot (root). Hankak = legs. Do not confuse with oina (foot specifically). Hanka sartu = to put your foot in it (to blunder).
Shoulder. Sorbaldak = shoulders. Sorbalda zabal = broad-shouldered. Used in expressions about responsibility.
Hamar (10) + ika. Hamaika also means countless/many in colloquial Basque: hamaika aldiz = countless times!
The single most useful phrase for a language learner. Mesedez errepikatu = please repeat. Poliki esaidazu = say it slowly.
Essential emergency phrase. Euskaraz hitz egiten duzu? = Do you speak Basque? The -z suffix means in that language.
The most useful tourist phrase. Non dago geltokia? = Where is the station? Non daude komunak? = Where are the toilets?
Dark blue. Itsas urdina = sea blue. The Basque flag (ikurrina) uses a vivid green cross on white diagonal bands on red - no dark blue, but urdina iluna appears in Basque clothing.

example.english: I am wearing a dark blue shirt.

vocabulary.notes: Sky blue. Argi = light as a modifier. Basque builds colour shades with argia (light) or iluna (dark). Zeruko urdina = sky blue (literally sky's blue).

example.english: The sky is light blue today.

vocabulary.notes: Dark yellow or gold. Urre kolorea = gold colour (from urre = gold). Used for describing autumn leaves in the Basque mountains in October.

example.english: The leaves are golden in autumn.

vocabulary.notes: Greenish or olive. The -xka suffix means somewhat/a bit - a very productive Basque diminutive used to describe approximate colours and sizes.
Cream or off-white. Compound colours in Basque are formed by combining two colour words. Zuri = white + horia = yellow = cream. Very common for describing walls and fabrics.
Beech tree - the most iconic tree of the Basque Country. Pago forests (pagadiak) cover the mountains. Many place names include Pago-: Pagasarri, Pagoeta. The autumn colours are spectacular.

example.basque: Pagoa oso ohikoa da Euskal Herrian.
example.english: The beech tree is very common in the Basque Country.

vocabulary.notes: Oak - sacred in Basque culture. The Gernikako Arbola (Tree of Gernika) is an ancient oak under which Basque assemblies met. Haritz = oak root. Symbol of Basque democracy and identity.

example.english: The oak is a symbol of the Basque Country.

vocabulary.notes: Lake. Aintzira is the standard literary word. The Basque Country has several mountain lakes including Urkulu and Gorbeia lakes. Different from itsasoa (the sea).
Mountain range. Mendi = mountain + lerroa = row/line. The Pyrenees (Pirinioak) form the natural border. The Cantabrian range (Kantauri mendilerroa) runs along the coast.
Ecosystem. Ekologia = ecology. The Basque Country spans Atlantic, mountain, and near-Mediterranean ecosystems in a small area. Biodiversity protection is a key political issue.
Biodiversity. The Basque Pyrenees host brown bears (hartzak), griffon vultures, and rare orchids. Biodiversity loss is a major concern in Basque environmental politics.
50 = two-twenties + ten. Berrogei = 40 (two twenties) + ta (and) + hamar (10) = 50. The vigesimal system: 20, 40, 60, 80 are the key round numbers in Basque.
60 = three twenties (hiru=3 + hogei=20). The vigesimal system: laurogei=80 (four twenties). French also uses this system: soixante (60) = six-tens, quatre-vingts (80) = four-twenties.
90 = four-twenties + ten. Laurogei=80, laurogeita hamar=90. The complete round numbers: hogei=20, berrogei=40, hirurogei=60, laurogei=80, ehun=100.
Second (ordinal). The -garren suffix makes ordinals: bigarrena=2nd, hirugarrena=3rd, laurgarrena=4th. Exception: lehena (not batgarrena) for first. Bigarren postua = second place.
Third (ordinal). Hirugarren aldiz = for the third time. Hirugarren mailan = at the third level. The -garren suffix is very regular making Basque ordinals easy once you know the cardinals.
Nephew or niece - same word for both genders in Basque. Context clarifies. Ilobak = nephews/nieces. Another example of Basque gender-neutrality in family terms.
Cousin. Lehen = first. A common word in extended Basque families. Lehengusina = female cousin in some dialects. Basque family networks (senitartea) are traditionally very close.
Relatives or extended family. Senide = relative/sibling. Basque families (etxeak) traditionally had strong clan identities tied to the family house. Senitartekoak = family members.
Widow or widower - same word for both genders. Alarguna geratu = to be widowed. Another example of Basque gender-neutral vocabulary. Alarguntasuna = widowhood.

example.english: She has been a widow for a long time.

vocabulary.notes: Adoption. Adoptatu = to adopt. Seme-alaba adoptatu = adopted child. Basque law has progressive adoption rules. The concept of chosen family (aukeratutako familia) is valued.
OK or agreed. From Spanish de acuerdo. Ados nago = I agree. Ados gaude = we are agreed. One of the most used words in everyday Basque conversation across all age groups.
Maybe or perhaps. Agian etorriko naiz = maybe I will come. More hopeful than beharbada. Used constantly in everyday speech - a very characteristic Basque filler word.

example.basque: Agian bihar eguraldi ona izango da.
example.english: Maybe tomorrow the weather will be good.

vocabulary.notes: Congratulations or happy birthday! Zorion = happiness/felicity (a single root, not zori+on). Zorioneko = lucky/happy. Zorionez = luckily/fortunately. One of the most joyful Basque words to say and hear.
Take care - a warm farewell. Ondo = well + ibili = walk/go around. Literally walk well. More affectionate than agur. Used among friends. Often said when parting for a while.
Thank you for everything. Dena = everything + -gatik = for/because of. Used at the end of a visit or celebration. A warm, complete expression of deep gratitude.

example.basque: Mila esker denagatik, oso ondo pasa dugu.
example.english: Thank you for everything, we had a great time.

vocabulary.notes: The Basque flag - green cross on white diagonal on red background. Designed in 1894 by the Arana brothers. Ikur = symbol. One of the most recognised regional flags in Europe.

example.english: We have the Basque flag at home.

vocabulary.notes: Cider house - a Basque institution. Open January-April. You eat bacalao omelette and txuleta (steak) and drink cider straight from the barrel when Txotx! is called. A must-do experience.

example.english: We are going to the cider house!

vocabulary.notes: The Basque tradition of going bar to bar eating pintxos and drinking. Common on Thursday and Friday evenings in many towns. Pote = drink. A cornerstone of Basque social life that brings communities together.

example.english: We will do pintxo-pote on Thursday evening.

vocabulary.notes: Ancient Basque percussion instrument - two players beat wooden planks together in complex rhythms. Used at cider pressings and festivals. A unique sound found nowhere else in the world.

example.english: The txalaparta players are wonderful to watch.

vocabulary.notes: Basque Homeland Day - celebrated on Easter Sunday. Aberri = homeland + eguna = day. Major political and cultural celebration with demonstrations across the Basque Country and diaspora.

example.english: There is a demonstration on Basque Homeland Day.

vocabulary.notes: Pride (noun). Harro = proud (adjective). Harrotasunez = with pride. Do not confuse with harrokeria (arrogance/vanity) which is negative. Healthy pride in identity is central to Basque culture.
Loneliness or solitude. Bakar = alone (root). In Basque culture, solitude in nature (mendian bakarrik) is often valued positively. Bakardadean = in solitude. A contemplative word.
Tears (plural). Malko = tear (singular). Malkoak isuri = to shed tears. Malkotan = in tears. Basque poetry and song have beautiful expressions involving tears and longing.
Overjoyed or full of joy. Poz = joy + betea = full/filled. Literally joy-filled. A more intense form of pozik (happy). Poz-betean = overjoyed. Basque can compound emotions this way.

example.english: I am overjoyed with this news.

vocabulary.notes: Democracy. Demokratikoa = democratic. Basque foral democracy under the Tree of Gernika is one of Europe's oldest traditions. The Basque Parliament has its seat in Gasteiz (Gasteiz (Vitoria)).
Elections (plural). Hautatu = to choose. Hauteskunde orokorrak = general elections. The Basque Country holds its own elections for the Basque Parliament, separate from Spanish national elections.
Equality. Berdin = equal/same. Genero-berdintasuna = gender equality (major topic in Basque politics). Berdintasunezko = egalitarian. The Basque Country has progressive equality legislation.
Rights (plural). Esku = hand + bide = path = rights! Oinarrizko eskubideak = fundamental rights. Hizkuntza eskubideak = language rights - a central issue in Basque politics and daily life.
Calendar. Egun = day + -tegi = place. Literally day-holder. Egutegian markatu = to mark on the calendar. Euskal egutegia includes traditional Basque festivals and celebrations.

example.english: I need to look at the calendar.

vocabulary.notes: Urtaroak = seasons (plural). Urtaroa = season (singular). Udaberria=spring, Uda=summer, Udazkena=autumn, Negua=winter. The Basque Country is known for changeable weather - four seasons sometimes in one day.
Spring. Uda = summer + berria = new. Literally new summer. Spring arrives later in the Basque mountains. Udaberrian = in spring. Flowers (loreak) bloom in the valleys from March.
Summer. Udan = in summer. Uda-oporretan = on summer holidays. Basque summers are mild - rarely very hot due to Atlantic influence. Donostia (San Sebastián)'s beaches are packed in July and August.

example.english: In summer we go to the sea.

vocabulary.notes: Autumn. Uda = summer + azkena = last. Literally last summer. Udazkenean = in autumn. The beech forests turn golden. Mushroom season (perretxiko garaia) begins in October.
Winter. Neguan = in winter. Neguak = winters. Snow (elurra) in the mountains, rain on the coast. Ski resorts operate in the Pyrenees. Neguko jaiak = winter festivals.
Colleague or coworker. Lan = work + kidea = companion. Lankide ona = good colleague. Lankidego = staff/team. Essential word for anyone working in a Basque-speaking environment.
Appointment or meeting. Hitz = word + ordua = hour. Literally word-time - a time to keep your word. Hitzordua eman = to give an appointment. Also used for doctor appointments.
Report or document. Txostena idatzi = to write a report. Urteko txostena = annual report. Very common in professional Basque - government and business documents are required in Basque.

example.english: I need to prepare the report.

vocabulary.notes: Contract. Kontratua sinatu = to sign a contract. Lan-kontratua = work contract. Aldi baterako kontratua = temporary contract. Key vocabulary for job seekers and HR professionals.
Strange or unusual. Arraro = strangely. Arraroa iruditzen zait = it seems strange to me. More negative than bitxia (curious/interesting). Arrotasuna = strangeness.
Curious, interesting or slightly odd - in a positive way. Bitxi = jewel (also!). Bitxikeriak = curiosities. More positive than arraroa. Zer bitxia! = How interesting! Common in conversation.
Tiring or exhausting. Neka + -garri (causing). The -garri suffix: interesgarria=interesting, harrigarria=amazing, nekagarria=tiring. Once you know -garri you can understand many adjectives.
Unbearable. Jasan = to bear/endure + ezin = cannot. Literally cannot-be-endured. Jasangarria = bearable (opposite). Shows how Basque systematically builds complex meanings from roots.
Essential or indispensable. Ezin = cannot + beste = other. Literally cannot-be-otherwise. A key word in formal writing, speeches, and political discourse in the Basque Country.
Passport. Pasaportea galdu dut = I have lost my passport. Pasaportea berritu = to renew a passport. Spain is in the Schengen Area - EU visitors do not need a visa.
Tourism. Turista = tourist. Turismo bulegoa = tourist office. The Basque Country receives millions of tourists for food culture, the Guggenheim Bilbo (Bilbao), and the Camino de Santiago.
Shelter or refuge. Mountain shelters (mendiko aterpeak) are essential for hikers. The GR11 and GR10 long-distance paths cross the Basque Pyrenees with huts every few hours.
Hamar (10) + hiru (3) = 13. The teen numbers follow this pattern: hamabi=12, hamahiru=13, hamalau=14, hamabost=15, hamasei=16, hamazazpi=17, hamazortzi=18, hemeretzi=19.
Hamar (10) + lau (4) = 14. Once you know the single digits and hamar, all Basque teen numbers follow perfectly: hamar + digit.
40 = two twenties (bi + hogei). The vigesimal system: hogei=20, berrogei=40, hirurogei=60, laurogei=80. French also counts this way: quatre-vingts = 80.
70 = three-twenties + ten. Hirurogei (60) + ta (and) + hamar (10) = 70. The complete round numbers in Basque vigesimal: 20,40,60,80,100.
80 = four twenties (lau=4 + hogei=20). Compare: French quatre-vingts = 80. The Basque and Breton vigesimal systems may share ancient roots.
Hamar (10) + sei (6) = 16. Hamasei sounds like 'hammer-say'. The teen series: hamabi=12, hamahiru=13, hamalau=14, hamabost=15, hamasei=16.
Hamar (10) + zazpi (7) = 17. Note the zz = th sound (like English 'this'). Hamazazpi is one of the harder teen numbers to pronounce.
Hamar (10) + zortzi (8) = 18. Hamazortzi is the longest teen number. The -zortzi ending has the same -tzi you see in zortzi (8).
19 = hemeretzi. Note: this does NOT follow the hamar+digit pattern! Hemeretzi is an irregular form. Compare French dix-neuf (10+9) vs Basque irregular hemeretzi.

Vegetable. Barazki = vegetable (root). Barazkiak = vegetables (plural). Barazki salda = vegetable broth. The Basque markets (merkatuak) are famous for fresh local vegetables, especially peppers and tomatoes.
Meat. Haragi = meat (root). Txuleta = steak (a Basque speciality). Txerri haragia = pork. Behi haragia = beef. Haragia erretzea = grilling meat. The txuleta at a sagardotegi is legendary.

Pig or pork. Txerri haragia = pork. Txistorra = Basque spicy sausage made from pork and paprika - eaten at every festival. Txerri-festa = pig festival, a traditional winter celebration in rural areas.
Oil. Oliba olioa = olive oil - essential in Basque cooking. Olioa berotzea = to heat the oil. Olio = oil root. Used in pil-pil sauce, the famous Basque emulsified cod dish.

Garlic. Baratxuri = garlic (root). Indispensable in Basque cooking - in salsa verde, pil-pil, and almost every sauce. Baratxuri ale bat = one clove of garlic. Often fried in oil first to flavour a dish.

example.english: I have added garlic to the sauce.

vocabulary.notes: Pepper. Piperrak = peppers (plural). Piper gorria = red pepper. Piper berdea = green pepper. The piperada (Basque pepper and tomato stew) is a classic dish. Gernikako piperrak = Gernika peppers, small green peppers unique to the Basque Country.

Potato. The same word as Spanish patata. Patata tortilla = potato omelette, ubiquitous in Basque bars. Patata frijituak = fried potatoes. The Basque Country grows distinctive small potatoes in the interior valleys.

example.english: The omelette is made with potato.

vocabulary.notes: Rice. From Spanish arroz via Arabic ar-ruzz. Arrozarekin = with rice. Arroz con leche (arrozesnea) = rice pudding. Less central than in Spanish cuisine but used in Basque seafood dishes.

Prestatu = to prepare or get ready. Afaria prestatu = to prepare dinner. Janaria prestatu = to prepare food. Also used outside cooking: bidaia prestatu = to prepare a trip. Very common verb in everyday Basque.

To eat - one of the most essential verbs. Jan = eat (also used as noun: janaria = food). Zer jan duzu? = What did you eat? Jaten ari naiz = I am eating. Jan eta edan = eat and drink. The j is a y sound.

example.english: What do you want to eat?

vocabulary.notes: To drink. Zer edan nahi duzu? = What do you want to drink? Edaten ari naiz = I am drinking. Jan eta edan = eat and drink. Edaria = drink (noun). Edateko ura = drinking water.

example.english: What do you want to drink?

vocabulary.notes: Eye (singular). Begiak = eyes (plural). Begi urdin = blue eye. Begi on = good eye / lucky charm. Begiratu = to look. Begikoa = attractive/pleasing. Begi onez ikusi = to look favourably on something.
Neck. Lepo = neck (root). Lepokoa = necklace (neck-thing). Lepoa mindu = stiff neck. Lepora hartu = to put on one's shoulders (to take responsibility). Lepo arte! = up to the neck = very busy.
Knees (plural). Belaun = knee (singular). Belaunetan = on one's knees. Belaunikatu = to kneel. Belaunaldi = generation (knee-age = a kneeling span of time - a lovely etymology).
Fingers (plural). Hatz = finger (singular). Oinhatza = toe (foot-finger). Hatz erakuslea = index finger (pointing finger). Hatz txikia = little finger. Hatzaz erakutsi = to point at.

example.english: I need to clean my fingers.

vocabulary.notes: Maskuria = bladder (gernu-maskuria = urinary bladder). Also in traditional culture: maskuri dantza = bladder dance, a Basque carnival tradition. Do not confuse with sabela (stomach/belly) or sabelaldea (abdominal area).

example.english: I have problems with my bladder.

vocabulary.notes: Liver. Gibel = liver (root). Medically important and culturally interesting - gibela atzetik = from behind/at the back (gibelera = backwards). Basque cuisine features foie gras (pato gibela) in fine dining.

example.english: I need to get my liver checked.

vocabulary.notes: Lung. Birikak = lungs (plural). Biriketako gaixotasuna = lung disease. Birika osasuntsuak = healthy lungs. Birika beteak = full lungs. Important medical vocabulary for healthcare settings.
Bird. Txori = bird (root). Txoria kaiolan = the bird in a cage - the title of the famous Basque song by Mikel Laboa, iconic in Basque culture. Txoritegi = aviary. Txori kantua = birdsong.

example.english: The bird is in the tree.

vocabulary.notes: Sheep. Ardi = sheep (root). Central to Basque rural life - Idiazabal cheese (Idiazabal gazta) is made from sheep's milk. Artzaina = shepherd. The Basque shepherd tradition is ancient and still alive in the Pyrenees.

example.english: The sheep are in the mountains.

vocabulary.notes: Cow. Behi = cow (root). Behi esnea = cow's milk. Behi haragia = beef. Behiak Basque farms are typical in the green valleys. Behiaren = of the cow.

example.english: The cows are in the meadow.

vocabulary.notes: Wolf. Otso = wolf (root). Wolves returned to the Basque Pyrenees in recent decades - a controversial topic. In Basque mythology the wolf (otso) appears in many legends. Otsaila = February (wolf-month - when wolves were most active).

example.english: The wolf lives in the forest.

vocabulary.notes: Bear. Hartz = bear (root). Brown bears (hartz arrea) survive in the Pyrenees near the Basque Country. Highly protected and controversial with farmers. Hartzak = bears (plural). Hartzgune = bear territory.

example.english: The bear lives in the Pyrenees.

vocabulary.notes: Bat. Sagu = mouse + zar(ra) = old = old mouse! One of the most charming Basque etymologies. Saguzarrak = bats (plural). Gaueko hegaztia = night flyer. Saguzarrek intsektuak jaten dituzte = bats eat insects.
Frog. Igel = frog (root). Common in Basque streams and wetlands. Igela uretatik kanpo = out of water (like a fish out of water). Igelaren saltoa = the frog's jump.

example.english: The frog is near the river.

vocabulary.notes: Divorce. Dibortziatu = to divorce. Dibortziatua = divorced. From Spanish divorcio. Spain legalised divorce in 1981 - relatively recent. Basque divorce rates are similar to European averages.
Parents (plural). Guraso = parent (singular). Gurasoek = by the parents. Guraso ezkondugabeak = unmarried parents. Gurasoen etxea = the parents' house. One of the most important family words.
Child or baby. Haur = child (root). Haurtzaro = childhood. Haurra zaintzea = to look after a child. Haur eskola = nursery school. Haurrak = children (plural). Haurrak behar ditu = she needs children.

Young or a young person. Gazte = young (root). Gazteak = young people. Gazteria = youth (collective). Gaztetxea = youth centre (young-house). Gazteakuasoa = when they were young. Gaztetasuna = youth/youthfulness.

Frustration or deep resentment. Sumitu = to be offended or irritated. Sumin = irritation. Sumigarria = irritating/infuriating. A stronger word than haserre (anger) - suggests a simmering resentment rather than acute anger.

Regret or remorse. Damutu = to regret. Damuz = with regret. Damua sentitu = to feel remorse. Damu naiz = I am sorry/I regret it. More profound than barkatu (excuse me) - damua involves genuine remorse.

Love (noun). Maitatu = to love. Maitea = loved one/beloved. Maite zaitut = I love you. Maitasunezko = of love/loving. Maitasuna is the abstract noun - compare with maite (the adjective/verb). Central to Basque poetry and song.

Warm or lukewarm. Epel = lukewarm (root). Between beroa (hot) and hotza (cold). Epelak = mild weather. Ura epela = lukewarm water. Epeltasuna = warmth. Basque weather is often described as epela - mild and Atlantic.
Naked or bare. Biluz = naked (root). Biluzi = to undress. Biluzirik = naked (adverb). Mendi biluzia = bare mountain. Used both literally and figuratively - egia biluzia = the naked truth.

example.english: The tree is bare in winter.

vocabulary.notes: Lively, vivid, or intense (adjective). Do not confuse with bizi (to live, verb). Bizi-bizia = very lively. Kolore bizia = vivid colour. Musika bizia = lively music. Bizitasuna = liveliness. The same root gives us bizitza (life) and bizirik (alive).

Ancient or from ancient times. Antzina = long ago. Antzinatekoa = of antiquity. The Basque language itself is often described as antzinatekoa - one of the oldest languages in Europe with no known relatives.

A little or a bit. Pixka = small amount. Pixka bat gehiago = a little more. Euskara pixka bat badakit = I know a little Basque. The most useful phrase for a beginner - shows humility and willingness to try.

Slowly or gently. Poliki hitz egin = speak slowly please. Poliki-poliki = very slowly/little by little. Also means nicely or gently. Poliki esaidazu = say it slowly to me. Essential for learners asking native speakers to slow down.
Again or once more. Berriz esan = say again. Berriz etorri = come again. Berriz ere = once again/yet again. Berriro = again (variant). Essential for language learners who need things repeated.

I know. Ba- is an affirmative prefix + dakit (I know, from jakin). Ez dakit = I don't know. Badakizu? = Do you know? Bai, badakit = Yes, I know. The ba- prefix adds affirmation and is very characteristic of Basque.

vocabulary.english: To grill / To roast / To smoke
vocabulary.notes: Erre = to grill, roast, or smoke. Txuleta errea = grilled steak. Erretegia = grill restaurant. Erre also means to burn or to smoke cigarettes. Egurra erre = to burn wood. The rr = rolled r.
Egosi = to boil or cook. Arrautza egosia = boiled egg. Barazkiak egosi = boil the vegetables. Egosi ongi = cook well. A fundamental cooking verb alongside erre (grill) and frijitu (fry).
Frijitu = to fry. Arrautza frijitua = fried egg. Frijitua = fried (adjective). Patata frijituak = chips/fries. From Spanish freír. Frijitegian = in the fryer. Very common in Basque bar cooking.

example.english: I want to fry an egg.

vocabulary.notes: Gatza = salt. Gatz = salt (root). Gazia = salty. Gazta = cheese literally means salted thing! Gatzontzia = salt cellar. Salt was historically precious - Basque salt trade routes were important in medieval times.

example.english: You need to add salt to it.

vocabulary.notes: Azukrea = sugar. From Arabic as-sukkar via Spanish azúcar. Azukre = sugar (root). Azukre gehiegi = too much sugar. Gozoa = sweet - a word often associated with azukrea.

example.english: Do you want sugar in your coffee?

vocabulary.notes: Merluzea = hake, the most popular fish in Basque cooking. Merluza salda = hake broth. Merluzea salsa berdean = hake in green sauce (salsa verde) - a classic Basque dish. From Spanish merluza.

example.english: I ate hake in green sauce.

vocabulary.notes: Txuleta = steak or chop. The txuleta de vaca (beef txuleta) is legendary in Basque cuisine - a thick bone-in ribeye from old dairy cows, grilled over charcoal. A centrepiece of sagardotegi meals.

example.basque: Txuleta erreak ongi eginda nahi dut.
example.english: I want the steak well done.

vocabulary.notes: Esnegaina = cream (also written esne-gaina). Esne = milk + gaina = top. Literally the top of the milk. Confirmed by Elhuyar corpus. Esnegain urtu = melted cream. Used in Basque desserts including goxua and intxaursaltsa. Krema is also used in modern Basque.

Intxaursaltsa = traditional Basque walnut dessert sauce. Intxaur = walnut + saltsa = sauce. Made from walnuts, milk, sugar, and cinnamon (kanela). Eaten warm at Christmas Eve dinner - over 150 years old. One of the oldest Basque desserts, from Gipuzkoa.
Ukondoa = elbow. Ukondo = elbow (root). Ukondoaz jo = to elbow/nudge. Ukondo min dut = my elbow hurts. In Basque ukondoa is also used metaphorically - ukondoka = by elbowing through.
Eskumuturra = wrist. Esku = hand + mutur = snout/tip. Literally the tip of the hand - a vivid description. Eskumutur min = wrist pain. Erlojua eskumuturran = watch on the wrist.
Bularraldea = chest. Bular = chest/breast (root). Bularra = chest. Bularreko min = chest pain. Bularreko hezurra = rib. Bularkoi = brave (chest-strong). Bularrez = face to face.
Azala = skin or cover/surface. Azal = skin/peel/bark/cover (root). Fruitaren azala = fruit skin/peel. Liburuaren azala = book cover. Azaleko = superficial/surface-level. A very versatile root word.
Odola = blood. Odol = blood (root). Odoltsu = bloody. Odol taldea = blood type. Odol-emaile = blood donor. Odol beroa = hot-blooded/passionate. Odolkia = blood sausage (a Basque delicacy).

Hezurra = bone. Hezur = bone (root). Hezurra hautsi = to break a bone. Hezurdura = skeleton. Hezurretaraino = to the bone. Gazta ondua = aged cheese (to the bone = very aged). Common in medical contexts.

Giltzurrunak = kidneys (plural). Giltzurrun = kidney (singular). Taught as plural since kidneys always come in pairs. Giltzurruneko harriak = kidney stones. Gernu-giltzurrunak = renal/urinary kidneys. Key medical vocabulary.
Suhia = son-in-law. Suhi = son-in-law (root). Confirmed by Elhuyar: suhi-errain = son-in-law and daughter-in-law collectively. Suhia etxera etorri da = the son-in-law has come home.

Erraina = daughter-in-law. Errain = daughter-in-law (root). Confirmed by Elhuyar: suhi-errain = son-in-law and daughter-in-law pair. Senarreba = in-laws collectively. Erraina etxean bizi da = the daughter-in-law lives at home.

example.english: The daughter-in-law is a new mother.

vocabulary.notes: Bortxa = violence or force. Bortxakeria = violence (repeated). Etxeko bortxa = domestic violence. Bortxatu = to force/violate. An important word in social contexts, especially domestic abuse awareness campaigns.
Bakea = peace. Bake = peace (root). Bakean bizi = to live in peace. Bake hitzarmena = peace agreement. Bake prozesua = peace process (very relevant to Basque history post-ETA). Baketsu = peaceful.

example.english: We want to live in peace.

vocabulary.notes: Alaitasuna = happiness or cheerfulness (noun). Alai = cheerful (adjective). Alaigarri = cheering. Zer alaitasun! = What happiness! A warm, active form of happiness - more about being lively than content.
Kezka = worry or concern (noun). Kezkatu = to worry (verb). Kezkagarria = worrying. Kezkaz = with worry. Kezka nagusia = main concern. One of the most common emotion words in everyday Basque speech.
Nahigabea = disappointment or grief. Nahi = want + gabe = without = wanting-without = lacking what you want. Nahigabeturiko = disappointed/grieved. A more literary sadness than tristura.

vocabulary.english: To cheer up / To make happy
vocabulary.notes: Poztu = to cheer up or make happy. Pozten naiz = I am glad/pleased. Pozten nau = it pleases me. Poztu naiz zure berria entzutean = I was delighted to hear your news. From poz (joy).

example.english: I am happy to see you.

vocabulary.notes: Urrea = gold (colour and metal). Urrezko = golden. Urre kolorea = gold colour. Urregorria = golden-red. Basque gold craftsmanship (urregintza) has ancient roots - gold torques from pre-Roman times found in the Basque region.
Urdinaxka = bluish or blue-grey. The -xka suffix makes approximate colours: berdexka=greenish, urdinaxka=bluish. The Basque sky in autumn is often described as urdinaxka - a misty blue-grey.

Gorrigorria = bright red or scarlet. Reduplication intensifies colour in Basque: gorri=red, gorrigorri=very red. Same pattern: zuri-zuria=pure white, beltz-beltza=jet black. A very expressive feature of the language.

Zerumuga = horizon. Zeru = sky + muga = border/limit. Literally the sky-border. A beautiful compound. Zerumuga urrunean = on the far horizon. Common in Basque poetry and descriptions of the sea coast.
Itsasertza = coastline. Itsas = sea + ertz = edge. The Basque coastline is dramatic - rocky cliffs (labarrak), sandy beaches (hondartzak), and fishing harbours (portuan). Itsasertzeko = coastal.

Haitzak = cliffs or rocks (plural). Haitz = rock/cliff (singular). Haitzulo = cave (cliff-hole). The Basque coast is famous for its haitzak - dramatic limestone cliffs. Haitz-arte = between the rocks.

Larrea = meadow or pasture. Larre = pasture (root). Larre = to pasture animals. The Basque interior is covered in green meadows (larreak) where sheep and cattle graze. Many place names include -larre.

example.english: The sheep are in the meadow.

vocabulary.notes: Labarrak = sea cliffs (plural). Labar = cliff (singular). The Basque coastline has spectacular labarrak, especially around Zumaia with its flysch geological formations - millions of years of earth history visible in the cliff faces.
Egia esan = to tell the truth, or as a filler: honestly/frankly. Egia esan, ez dakit = honestly, I don't know. Egia = truth. Esan = to say. Egiazki = truly/indeed. Very common in Basque conversation.

example.basque: Egia esan ez dakit zer egin.
example.english: Honestly I don't know what to do.

vocabulary.notes: Hala ere = nevertheless or even so. Hala = so/thus + ere = also/even. Essential for expressing concession. Zaila da hala ere saiatuko naiz = It is hard but I will try anyway. Very common connector in speech.
Hori da! = That's it! / Exactly! / That's right! A very common affirmation. Hori da! Ongi etorri! = That's it! Welcome! Also: Hori bai! = Now that's something! Hori ez = That's not it.
Gizena = fat or thick. Gizen = fat (root). Gizendu = to fatten/get fat. Gizeneria = obesity. Gizen-gizena = very fat. Used for people, animals, and objects (liburu gizena = thick book). Be tactful!
Argala = thin or slim. Argal = thin (root). Argaldu = to lose weight/become thin. Argaltasuna = thinness. Do not confuse with argia (bright/clever). Oso argala = very thin.
Osasuntsua = healthy. Osasun = health + -tsu (full of) + -a (article). Literally full of health. Elikagai osasuntsuak = healthy foods. Osasuntsuki = healthily. The opposite is gaixoa (sick/ill).

Gaixoa = sick, ill, or poor thing. Gaixotu = to fall ill. Gaixotasuna = illness. Gaixorik nago = I am ill. But gaixoa also expresses sympathy: gaixoa! = poor thing! A word with two very different uses.
Nagikeria = laziness. Nagi = lazy (adjective). Nagitu = to become lazy. Nagikeriak jota = struck by laziness. Nagi egon = to laze around. The suffix -keria makes nouns for vices or bad habits.
Bihotzbera = kind-hearted or tender-hearted. Bihotz = heart + bera = soft/tender. Literally soft-hearted. Bihotzbera da = he/she is kind-hearted. A lovely compound showing Basque expressiveness.
Fourth (ordinal). Lau (4) + -garren + -a. Laurgarren aldia = the fourth time. Ordinals in Basque: lehena=1st, bigarrena=2nd, hirugarrena=3rd, laurgarrena=4th, bosgarrena=5th.
Fifth (ordinal). Bost (5) + -garren + -a. Bosgarren solairua = the fifth floor. The -garren suffix makes any Basque number into an ordinal. Exception: lehena (not batgarrena) for first.

example.english: I live on the fifth floor.

vocabulary.notes: Erdia = half. Erdi = half (root). Ordu erdia = half an hour. Erdiak = half past (telling time). Erdibitu = to halve. Erditik = from the middle. A very useful word - appears constantly in time expressions.
Bikoitza = double. Bi = two + -koitz (fold). Hirukoitza = triple. Bikoiztatu = to double. Bikoitza ordaindu = to pay double. The -koitz suffix: bikoitz=double, hirukoitz=triple, laukoitz=quadruple.
Gehiago = more. Geh = more (root). Gehiegi = too much. Gehiago nahi dut = I want more. Gehiago ez = no more. Pixka bat gehiago = a little more. One of the most used words in everyday Basque.
